advantages: Layered vocabulary (input / hidden / output, width, depth) gives a shared language for papers, code, and architecture diagrams across frameworks.
Expressing each layer as a matrix multiply enables efficient batched GPU computation and reuse of optimized linear algebra libraries.
Modular width and depth knobs let practitioners scale capacity gradually — add neurons or layers without redesigning the whole pipeline.
The neuron-as-unit model maps cleanly from single perceptrons to deep stacks, making the learning path from classical ML to deep nets continuous.
disadvantages: Fully connected layers with large width are parameter-heavy: connecting d_in to d_out costs d_in * d_out weights, which grows quickly for wide tiers.
Deeper stacks can be harder to train (vanishing/exploding signals) and slower to run than shallow wide nets for some tasks — depth is not free capacity.
Counting layers inconsistently (including input/output in depth, or counting activation modules as layers) causes communication bugs across teams.
Dense connectivity ignores spatial or sequential structure in raw data; convolutional or recurrent layers are often needed instead of stacking plain fully connected tiers.
